---
title: "Attention Is All You Need"
author: "Vaswani et al. (Google Brain)"
author_url: "https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762"
date: 2017-06-12
type: research
category: "Machine Learning"
tags: [transformers, attention, nlp, deep-learning]
source_url: "https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762"
estimated_read_time: 10
is_own_work: false
gists:
  quick: "The Transformer architecture — proposed in this 2017 paper — replaced recurrent and convolutional networks with a pure attention mechanism. By letting every token in a sequence attend directly to every other token in parallel, it enabled training on much larger datasets and became the foundation for GPT, BERT, and every large language model today. The key insight: attention is all you need — no recurrence, no convolutions."
  medium: "This 2017 paper introduced the Transformer architecture, which is now the foundation of virtually all state-of-the-art NLP and increasingly computer vision, audio, and multimodal AI systems.\n\nBefore Transformers, sequence modeling relied on RNNs (recurrent neural networks) and LSTMs, which process tokens sequentially — making them slow to train (can't parallelize) and limited in how far back they can 'remember' (vanishing gradients). CNNs could parallelize but had a limited receptive field.\n\nThe Transformer's key innovation is **multi-head self-attention**: every position in the sequence can directly attend to every other position in a single operation. This gives O(1) path length between any two positions (vs O(n) for RNNs), enabling the model to capture long-range dependencies effectively.\n\nThe architecture has two main parts:\n- **Encoder**: Maps input tokens to contextual representations. Each layer applies self-attention (attend to all input positions) + feedforward network.\n- **Decoder**: Generates output tokens autoregressively. Uses self-attention on outputs so far + cross-attention on encoder outputs.\n\nPositional encoding injects position information (since attention itself is permutation-invariant).\n\nThe paper showed state-of-the-art results on machine translation with far less training time than RNN baselines. But the bigger impact came later: BERT (encoder-only), GPT (decoder-only), and T5 (encoder-decoder) scaled the architecture with pre-training on massive text corpora, leading to the LLM revolution."
  full: "Published in 2017 by researchers at Google Brain and Google Research, 'Attention Is All You Need' is arguably the most influential machine learning paper of the last decade. It introduced the Transformer architecture that underpins GPT, BERT, T5, PaLM, Gemini, Claude, and virtually every state-of-the-art AI system today.\n\n## The Problem with Sequential Models\n\nBefore Transformers, the dominant architectures for sequence modeling were Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) and Long Short-Term Memory networks (LSTMs). These process tokens one at a time, left to right, maintaining a hidden state that summarizes everything seen so far.\n\nThis sequential computation has two critical problems:\n1. **No parallelism**: You can't process token n until you've processed tokens 1 through n-1. This makes training on long sequences extremely slow.\n2. **Vanishing gradients**: Information from early in the sequence must flow through many time steps to influence the loss, getting diluted or lost (despite LSTM's gating mechanisms).\n\nConvolutional approaches (ByteNet, ConvS2S) could parallelize but had a limited receptive field — the number of layers grows with the distance between tokens that need to interact.\n\n## The Transformer Architecture\n\nThe Transformer eliminates recurrence entirely. Its core insight is that **attention** — a mechanism that lets every output position look at every input position and weight them by relevance — can replace sequential processing.\n\n### Scaled Dot-Product Attention\n\nGiven queries Q, keys K, and values V:\n```\nAttention(Q, K, V) = softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d_k)) * V\n```\n\nEach query computes a dot product with all keys (measuring compatibility), scales by sqrt(d_k) to prevent vanishing gradients in softmax, applies softmax to get attention weights, then takes a weighted sum of values.\n\n### Multi-Head Attention\n\nRather than one attention function, they use h parallel attention heads, each with its own learned projections. This lets the model jointly attend to information from different representation subspaces — capturing syntactic and semantic relationships simultaneously.\n\n### The Encoder\n\nThe encoder is a stack of N=6 identical layers, each with:\n1. Multi-head self-attention (every token attends to all tokens in input)\n2. Position-wise feedforward network (two linear layers with ReLU)\n3. Residual connections + layer normalization around each sub-layer\n\n### The Decoder\n\nThe decoder similarly stacks N=6 layers, but with three sub-layers:\n1. Masked multi-head self-attention (can only attend to earlier positions — prevents cheating during training)\n2. Multi-head cross-attention over encoder output\n3. Position-wise feedforward network\n\n### Positional Encoding\n\nSince attention is permutation-invariant (the order of tokens doesn't matter to the mechanism itself), positional encodings are added to the input embeddings. The paper uses fixed sinusoidal encodings of different frequencies, enabling the model to extrapolate to sequence lengths longer than those seen during training.\n\n## Results\n\nOn WMT 2014 English-to-German translation, the Transformer achieved **28.4 BLEU**, more than 2 points above the previous best (including ensembles), while training in **3.5 days on 8 P100 GPUs** — compared to weeks for RNN baselines.\n\n## Why It Mattered Beyond Translation\n\nThe architecture's power comes from three properties:\n1. **Parallelizability**: All positions computed simultaneously — scales with compute\n2. **O(1) path length**: Any two positions can interact directly — captures long-range dependencies\n3. **Pre-training compatibility**: The same architecture could be pre-trained on massive unlabeled text (BERT, GPT) and fine-tuned, unlocking the era of transfer learning in NLP\n\nBERT (2018) took the encoder, pre-trained on masked language modeling. GPT (2018, 2019, 2020) took the decoder, pre-trained on next-token prediction. Both demonstrated that the Transformer, when pre-trained at scale, learns remarkably general representations.\n\nThe scaling laws paper (Kaplan et al., 2020) showed that Transformer performance improves predictably with compute, data, and parameters — giving the field a roadmap that led directly to GPT-3, PaLM, and the modern LLM landscape.\n\n## Key Takeaways\n- Replace sequential recurrence with parallel attention\n- Multi-head attention captures multiple types of relationships simultaneously\n- The architecture scales — more compute = better models\n- Foundation for GPT, BERT, and all modern LLMs"
---

The paper that introduced the Transformer architecture — foundation of all modern large language models. First published at NeurIPS 2017, it replaced recurrent and convolutional sequence models with a pure attention mechanism.

**Abstract (abridged):** The dominant sequence transduction models are based on complex RNNs or CNNs, with an encoder and decoder connected through an attention mechanism. We propose the Transformer, a model based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions entirely. Experiments show these models to be superior in quality while being more parallelizable and requiring significantly less time to train.

## Core Contribution

Multi-head self-attention: every token can attend to every other token in the sequence in parallel. This gives O(1) connection distance between any two positions, enabling long-range dependency capture that RNNs struggle with.

## Architecture

- **Encoder**: N stacked layers, each with self-attention + feedforward + residual connections
- **Decoder**: Same structure + masked self-attention + cross-attention on encoder output
- **Positional encoding**: Sinusoidal encodings injected into embeddings (attention is permutation-invariant)

## Results

28.4 BLEU on WMT 2014 EN→DE, trained in 3.5 days on 8 GPUs. Significantly faster and better than all previous models including ensembles.
